title: execute_list_files lacks sort by type, pagination, and metadata (single-line dump)
description:
## Severity
Minor

## Dimension
Functional Correctness / KISS / Usability

## Location
src/tools/file/list.rs:7-33 (execute_list_files)

## Violation
The tool is a one-paragraph dump:

```rust
for entry in entries.flatten() {
    let name = entry.file_name().to_string_lossy().to_string();
    let file_type = entry.file_type().map_or("", |ft| if ft.is_dir() { "/" } else { "" });
    items.push(format!("{name}{file_type}"));
}
items.sort();
(items.join("\n"), false)
```

Problems:
1. `entries.flatten()` silently discards per-entry errors (e.g. EACCES on a specific symlink) — the model gets a partial list with no indication of failure.
2. No pagination — listing a directory with 50k entries produces a 50k-line response, consuming the entire token budget.
3. Alphabetical sort mixes files and directories — every other CLI sorts dirs first.
4. No file metadata: no size, mtime, no distinction between symlinks and regular files. A model trying to decide which file to read has zero signal.
5. No filter args (no glob, no recursive option, no hidden-file toggle).

## Mandated refactor
1. Accept optional args: `pattern` (glob), `recursive: bool`, `max_entries: usize` (default 200), `show_hidden: bool` (default false).
2. Return a structured listing: dirs first then files, each line "name/  " or "name (Nbytes, mtime)".
3. Surface flatten errors via tracing::warn! and append "(N entries skipped due to errors)" if any.
4. Cap output at max_entries; on truncation return is_error or a clear "(showing N of M entries — use pattern to narrow)" suffix.

## Filed from QA review dispatch
